key_insight: A system-specific MACE model trained on spin-polarized SQS data substantially improves prediction of Fe-Ni alloy structure, elasticity, magnetism, and thermal trends, but still misses key magnetic-collapse physics governing phase stability.
engineering_relevance: For alloy design and phase-stability screening, targeted retraining on representative disordered states can outperform foundation models and yield more reliable thermomechanical predictions.
method: Built a reference dataset from spin-polarized PBE DFT on chemically disordered SQS Fe-Ni alloys.
Included bcc and fcc structures across compositions, plus volumetric and shear deformations.
Benchmarked a system-specific MACE-sqs model against several foundation MACE models, including Hubbard-U variants.
evidence_signal: MACE-sqs achieved 2.0 meV/atom validation error for energies and 24.3 meV/Å for forces, and showed the best overall agreement with DFT and experiment for equations of state, equilibrium volumes, elastic constants, and thermal expansion.
watch_item: All tested models incorrectly predict that the bcc-to-hcp transition pressure increases with Ni content, indicating unresolved high-pressure magnetic collapse and magnetoelastic effects.
paper_title: Can MACE Potentials Accurately Describe Magnetism and Phase Stability in Fe-Ni Alloys? A Systematic Benchmark
